Okay, so we're going to be talking about legal stuff. Now, this is a session which I talk about stuff that I have no knowledge of. I have some experience with but no real expertise in. That's my disclaimer. My disclaimer is this, get a lawyer. But we're going to go through some of the clauses and generally what they mean. There's one clause that I asked my attorney and he didn't even know. But it's a potentially damaging clause so you need to really check with your own attorney if you see it. It's the indemnification clause. We'll get to in a minute. Anyway, a contract is kind of like picking up a chicken at a bar. The questions that you're answering in the contract are very similar. What's your name? What kind of business are you? How are you doing business? Are you a corporation or are you an LLC? What's the name of the company? Some guys may insert the location of the company in this part of the contract. Next, I'm single or you. On this one, you have your representations. I'm not married. I'm not bound to anybody else. I can work for you. I really can. This could work. The contractor is not aware of any presently existing facts or circumstances or event, yada, yada, yada, that would give Rice any breach of any other agreement. Basically, we're both available. It's legal for him to hire me. It's legal for me to allow myself to be hired by this guy. Then there are representations where we're making statements about who we are, what we do, and that kind of stuff. Just read through these. These kinds of things are pretty common sense. It's pretty much boilerplate stuff. You've got to read carefully. If you don't understand any clause, schedule an hour with an attorney and have him walk you through the whole thing. Red line at first. Get a yellow highlighter. Anything you don't understand or that looks not good to you, just sit down with your attorney and ask him to explain all this stuff. Microphone? An hour of service online. A friend of mine did this cash in employment contract. You don't have to have an attorney on file. She just went online and for a monthly fee, she gets so many hours of use. You don't have to? Yeah. Marcella was saying without a microphone that you can go online and get consultation as well. If you want to do that, it's certainly going to be cheaper. My attorney, when I first signed up with him, charged me a $2,500 advanced retainer and then deducts whatever time I use from that. Then at some point, he'll ask me for another $2,500 or whatever. It's wonderful. If you want to go the way as cheap as possible with an attorney, that's fine. As you get going, you're going to want a relationship. You're going to want a guy who understands your business, knows your business. What we do is very different from what most consultants do and the way most businesses operate. Having an attorney who understands your business is a great idea and somebody you have a relationship with. I'll tell you how weird our business is. Tom Phillips was building a great company, but he couldn't get any bank to understand his business model. He had to buy his own bank to finance his receivables and to give himself loans. That's how weird this is. Having a relationship with an attorney makes a lot of sense. Not to belabor the point, but I'm going to belabor the point. I told you the story about security where a coin had bullion. I found out the guy was cheating his customers. I wrote one of the best sales letters I ever wrote that sold the Federal Trade Commission on not coming after me for any of this when I resigned. They didn't and they didn't even call me in for depositions. The other part of it though, that was partly because of my resignation letter, I'm sure. The other part of it was I had a contract that said that I wasn't responsible for anything that I wrote. I can write all I want to say anything I want to as long as I don't show it to anybody or use it to make money, I haven't committed any kind of crime. My contract made it very clear that it was up to the client to determine that everything they sent to their customers was in legal and regulatory compliance. It very clearly stated that if there was ever a problem, it was their problem and not mine. Very important clause, we're going to cover it in a minute, but there's not much more to cover. That's basically it. Tell them that other story that ended you up in New York with being questioned. Yeah. Because that's part of this. Yeah, it really is. Probably the worst month of my life. I got a phone call from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission. The Commodity Futures, the CFTC, is the pit bull of government regulatory agents. They make FDA, FTC, SEC look like pussycats. I mean, these guys are tough. And I get a call from them and they said, we would like you to voluntarily come and be deposed. And it was a very nice letter. It said, basically, I could pick the time and location and I could do it in Chicago or I could do it in Manhattan. And it was about one of my clients, somebody I had done some work for named Nick Garino. So, I called my attorney, Charlie, and he was in Manhattan. So I said, we'll do it in Manhattan. So one year to the day before the 9-11 tragedy, I'm on the 86th floor of the World Trade Center being deposed by the CFTC. And it turns out that what was happening, and this could happen to any one of you guys, I was selling a subscription to his newsletter. He was collecting the money, not delivering the product. I say, I would have no way of knowing this, right? But the product itself, I mean, after the first issue or so, but he was also doing something else. He was going through direct mail and emails to those people who had signed up and he was selling them a completely non-compliant commodities trading program. And so, now this guy, to avoid prosecution, moved to... Was it Nicaragua? And they couldn't get to him and he never came to the states and they didn't know exactly where he was. And so they needed to understand more about his business and that's why I was called in. But when you get a letter from the CFTC asking you to voluntarily do something, that is not voluntary. You do it or they're going to come after you next. So once again, I had a contract with him absolving me of any liability based on any infraction of a regulation or a law and clearly stated that he and he alone got to make the decision whether or not to present my copy to the marketplace based on his own understanding of the law and regulations. So, basically it cost me $20,000 in a month of worry to deal with that. But you just don't want to be there. So, this kind of stuff is important and it gets back to why it's important to have an attorney who understands your business. Okay, so next is what are you looking for? Who are you? Are we both okay to do this thing? Are we? No contract prohibits me from doing this. No contract prohibits you from doing this. What are you looking for? What am I supposed to do for you? So itemize the components and allow them to write you seven emails and write you a squeeze page and write you a sales page. What are you going to write? What is the job? I like to see a line in there that's not necessary, absolutely necessary, but I like to see some kind of line that says that this definition may change as the work goes on because it always does, right? And there should also be a rough schedule. When am I supposed to deliver this stuff? Everyone's on the same page. The best thing about contracts really isn't that it binds anybody. The best thing about contracts is that it just gets everyone on the same page. We all know what we're talking about. We all know what the job is. We're all agreeing to this. So there can't be a situation later on where you go, oh, I didn't know the deadline was that day. Well, it's in your freaking contract, you know? So this is really a major purpose of this. Like I said, I've never had to really act on any of these. What's in it for me if I do these things for you? You see the progression here? Who are you? Are we free? What do I have to do? What's in it for me? What are you going to pay me? And again, each of these needs, it has to have the fear advance and the incentive. And also, the next slide is when do I get paid? So now everyone's clear. You're not going to have that little surprise that I had when I found out I only got paid semi-annually. Very important. How long do I get paid? So, how long, okay, this clause will have stuff sometimes, one of my contracts right now has stuff that says that the client is free to change my copy in any way he wants to and use it any way he wants to, so long as Clayton gets paid. It's very important. There have been some fairly reputable companies in my industry who have hired copywriters, Kim Krause Schwann and Blinda Carlisle come to mind, who have hired copywriters who wrote copy for them. Then the client paraphrased the copy, basically, you know, went through word by word and paraphrased it and went out and said, well, this is our new control. So now they didn't have to pay the royalty anymore. Isn't that a nightmare? You're all excited because you got the control and the next thing you know, your own client has ripped you off. So that clause is really good to have in here, too. Talk to your attorney about that. Explain that that is a problem with some companies that needs to be very clear that this copy, any time it's used, you get your royalty. I think there's a slide about this, too, but just in case. The question comes up, who owns a copy? And the answer is the client. He owns a copy. You don't. But that's okay because you have a guarantee in your contract that every single time he uses it, you're going to get a royalty. Then how do I know you're not cheating? And this is, copywriters ask this all the time, it's not a big deal. It's in the contract. How do I know? Well, I know because to confirm the accuracy of the mailing, well, first of all, he's got to give me a mailing report at the end of each month that tells me how many names he mailed of my piece or if it's online, what use he's made of my piece and what the results were. Okay. And then to confirm the accuracy of this report, you have the right to demand an audit or post office receipts or other documentation that prove his numbers. Now, again, especially for the new writers, young writers in the room, it's not in your client's best interest. If you've got a controller, it's not in his best interest to screw you. Just isn't. Two things will happen. Number one, he loses you. Number two, he loses every copywriter that listens to you when you tell him that this guy screwed you, right? So it's not in their best interest to cheap out on your royalties. So it very rarely happens. I've had it happen twice in 42 years. So, you know, very rarely. All right. Here's the prenupt. Let's say you're working on this thing. This happened to me. I was working on a project. And I sent in the first draft copy and the crit came back and it was clear to me I was working with a Drilling moron. And I just said this isn't going to work, guys. These crits are idiotic and they'll kill the package. And I quit. All right. But I had a kill fee in my contract. It just basically said I got to keep the advance. And I love this because basically the minute I start working on this project, it's like the client offering me a free $12,500 thing if I just pick up the phone and fire him, you know. So that makes clients very polite, very friendly, very interested in helping you succeed, among other things, of course. But that's a typical kill fee. Your upfront fee will be paid in two parts. The first part is half and the second part is half. The first part paid when you begin the job or when they schedule your time. That's something to think about, okay? A lot of people sleepwalk through this and they'll say, well, I take half of my upfront fee when I start work. Why? You're reserving the time now. You're blacking that time out so that nobody else could get that time from you. You have no other source of income for that period of time. I want my first half of my advance now. The minute I say yes, okay? Because your name is now on my schedule and I can't sell that time to anyone else. So first money, please. Okay? Second money comes when the client accepts your final draft. And at that point, the client may say, you know what? We don't like this. You know, I don't see any way to save it. And so we're just not going to, we're not going to pay you and we're not going to use it. You get to keep that first half. It's still a kill fee, okay? That has happened to me never. You know, usually, you know, if they've gone, has it Wendy? I don't think a single time. Can't think of a single time. But we've had a couple of bizarre things happen where you did get to keep the kill fee. Yeah, but it's never happened that I wrote the copy and it wasn't used. That I can remember. But if it did happen and so rare, neither one of us can remember a specific instance. So very, very rare. But still, you're protected by your kill fee even if that happens. Yeah. Microphone for Carly. Carly. Oh, now? Yeah. It's important that you do stipulate that you get the balance of your payment when final draft is accepted by the client, not final design, or anything else. Because once you've turned that final copy in, you've done your job. Now, true, you're going to work with a designer to get it to finalize, but they can hold on to that copy for months before they get it designed so you don't want them holding on to the payment. And it's also good to say within two weeks, right? These CFOs are bastards, you know, they will hold your check till hell won't have it just to age the funds. You know, there's some companies that will hold invoices for 60 days or even 90 days. So there's another clause you can put in there that charges them interest if they take more than 30 days to pay you. I've done that too. So, you know, you'll pay me within 30 days of the completion of the copy, or you'll be liable for a 10% interest on the money until you do pay me. And of course, CFOs, they get that. They're all about aging funds, right? So they get that, they look at it and go, oh, we don't want to do that. To us, it's pennies, you know, but it's a big deal to them. Okay, next, who owns this stuff? Right? It's community property care, paragraph kind of. Who owns this stuff? Okay, well, we talked about that. The client owns it. But it just means that they, you know, all you say is you, you know, you get paid no matter what. No restriction on companies use of work. This is that clause I was telling you about. So long as contractor is compensated, that's the key phrase here. As said forth here, and the company shall not be restricted in any way from modifying reverse engineering or decompiling the work or creating, you know, look at that reverse engineering. What does that mean? What that means is they, if they do what Marcella did with my copy, you know, they take my copy, they deconstruct it, and then they rewrite it along the exact same outline. That's not legal for them to do. Okay, if they do that, they have to pay me a royalty. Okay. Okay, what do we call this relationship? We just live in together, we get married, what's the deal? So, it's really important to the client that you be clearly identified as an independent contractor. It has all kinds of implications for withholding taxes and, you know, all kinds, you know, and they have to do matching FICA and stuff like that. So, you know, they don't want to be caught in that kind of situation. So, now with Obamacare and everything, there's all kinds of health insurance questions. If somebody at the IRS or someone else determines you're an employee, then it just opens a whole rat's nest for the company. So, this is one of their favorite clauses. It's just fine, it protects you too, and I wouldn't worry too much about it. If your client offers to buy you a computer or to provide your desk or, you know, other key pieces of equipment, the IRS may look at that and say, no, you're an employee. And you need to be paying withholding tax, you know, withholding tax, you need to be covered by the insurance plan, you need all of this other stuff. So, you know, they want to be protected against that. So, always take cash, don't take a new computer or any other hard equipment. You can buy your own stuff. So, a lot of these clauses are just baloney, you know, like for example, contractor determines means and methods. Now, see, I know that's baloney because I'm going to take direction from the company, right? And the company knows that's baloney, but any judge looking at this will tell the IRS, well, look, you know, he's obviously not an employee, he's determining how he does stuff. So, you know, it's just what they do. The company has no control. See how this goes on and on? You know, it's like the biggest part of the contract, you know? So, confidentiality. This is also no big deal. It basically says, look, especially before the copy is used, you don't tell anybody about it. No details, please, you know? After it's used, nobody cares because everyone, you know, all the competitors have seen it and everything. But this is, you know, you just got to make sure that, you know, you're careful during that sensitive period. Now, this may extend to all kinds of periods, but the bottom line is the company won't care, you know? If they mailed the piece and a million people have seen it, they're not going to sue you because you told somebody you wrote it, you know? So, don't worry about that. Well, you can read this stuff, anything. I won't bore you with it. The next one is non-compete. Now, if somebody hands you a contract with this in it, you know, just, you know, quit work for the day, go to the bar and have fun because this is a gift. If this client wants you on a non-compete basis, this happened to me with Phillips. You guys know, what is it, the federal farm bank, where they pay you not to grow crops? You know, that's what this is. You know, so Phillips came to me and they said, we don't want you to write for anybody else. And this, of course, this is back in the early 90s, so when $10,000 was $10,000. And so we don't want you to write for anybody else. And I said, well, okay, I think maybe you can pay me $120,000 a year not to write for anybody else. And it's like getting paid not to grow crops, right? Free money. And so that's what they did. And so if you see a non-compete in a contract, it's time to go back to the negotiating table. I'll be more than happy to do this, but you're going to have to pay for it because, you know, I've spent years developing relationships with other clients. And if you're now going to, if you want to deprive me the right to work with those people, you're going to have to pay for that. And, you know, unless this is just a frivolous thing they've stuck in there, you'll have some negotiating room and you can, you can add some money to your bottom line with this. It was a huge win-win. Yeah. Not only was I doing, when I was doing very well for Phillips, for their competitors. See, every time you do a job, there's several things that happen. You're doing the job. But also you're, you're introducing some new ideas or some new technology or some new concepts in the marketing of the product the way you wrote the copy, right? So at that time, for example, one of those things would have been alternative health newsletters, you know? And I could have gone to Agora in a heartbeat and said, okay, let's start an alternative health newsletter. As it was, it took them like two years to figure it out. But so I could have made millions with that information at the time. So asking for a compensation for a non-compete is absolutely good. Microphone for Marcella. You can also carve a little niche out. One thing I did when I first started was, Schafer's wanted a non-compete for anything. And I said, well, I'm not going to do that because then I might as well just still be an in-house writer. You know, the whole idea is I want to grow my business. I want to do other things. So I said, look, Paris, I'll do health with him. I won't do option trading services. That doesn't compete with you. He's brilliant. It's going to help what I'm doing for you. So let me do that. And they're like, okay, that sounds fine. And then the next relationship I had was with the Motley Fool. And that was back in the day. Remember when they had the, we will die before we'll ever trade an option? Yeah. So I went to Bernie and I said, look, you share list swaps with the Motley Fool. You guys market to each other. They have this rule that they'll die before they'll ever trade options. So can I go right for them? And I'll just keep doing options for you. So there's ways to like slice and dice. That's not what you want. You know, if you, if you see an opportunity here to make, you know, 20, $30,000 a month for signing a non-compete, it could be a blanket non-compete. If you want to still write for health clients, but this is a financial client, you can do that. My non-compete with Weiss right now is a selected non-compete. So, and it's very, I made it very specific. Okay. The first draft said Clayton is not allowed to write for any financial company. And I said, no, you got to be more specific than that. And so they came back with Clayton is not allowed to write for any other financial publisher or publisher of financial newsletters. That's what it said. So I could theoretically work for Forbes because they publish a magazine, not a newsletter. I could technically, I can work for the Money Show, which I'm doing for free right now. But I could, I could work for the Money Show and make money with them legally because they do investment conferences. They don't do newsletters. So, so it's a non-compete that still gives me freedom to go make money with a lot of other people if I want to. Of course, I could do health and all that other stuff. So, but again, look at this as an opportunity. If it comes up, they value you enough that they want you to sign this. It's an opportunity for you to sweeten the pot. Okay. This is that responsibility for compliances. Keep you out of prison. I can't believe that that guy actually got 20 years. My boss got 20 years on the RICO Act. I mean, he's out now, you know, but it, it, because it happened in like the 80s or very early 80s. And so he's out, been out for a while. But I mean, that's serious stuff he was doing. And so, you know, when I found out about it, I got the heck out of there. Of course, that'd be my advice to you too. But this kind of a paragraph and don't take these paragraphs and just write your own contract. You need to go through a lawyer. Okay. Did everyone hear that? I am not a lawyer. Okay. And, but, you know, so don't take these, this stuff here and try to cobble together a contract with it. So anyway, responsibility for compliance. Make it clear that don't shoot me. I'm just a piano player. I just write the copy, you know, I don't determine if or when or to whom this material will be sent. I am not a lawyer. I have no knowledge of regulations, the regulations about this. Or even the law. And so, you know, don't shoot me. The company has to take ultimate responsibility. One time I had the clause written so the company was warranting to me that they would have everything I wrote passed through a compliance officer or an attorney. It was, it was lame. It was health resources. I had them warrant to me that they would have an FDA attorney review and approve everything I wrote. And it also said that I would make whatever changes were required after discussion with the compliance people. So that, that will help you a lot. Okay. This is, this is a tricky one that you definitely need to talk to your lawyer about. Generally, as I understand it, and I asked my attorney, I said help me with this one. I don't really get it. And he sent me an email that is completely unintelligible. Basically the way I understand it is hold harmless means if all hell breaks loose, I'm not going to sue my client over it. He's not going to sue me. We hold each other harmless, right? Indemnification as I, and usually there, it's held, it's written this way. Clayton will indemnify and hold harmless, wise research. And then the next paragraph, wise research will indemnify and hold harmless Clayton make peace. Indemnification is a little bit trickier because it obligates you to compensate your client. If you, if it says Clayton make peace indemnifies wise research, that means that, that if wise research gets sued, whether it's by an investor, who's pissed off about the product or a government agency, you know, that I have to pay their legal bills. And it's not really that simple, but that's as close as I could get to an accurate description. So make sure that you look very carefully. I won't sign a contract that says that I indemnify anybody, you know. And the lawyers will sometimes say, well, Clayton, you know, it's kind of mitigated over here, or we took care of it in another way. I said, no, no, Clayton doesn't indemnify anything, you know. If you guys get in trouble, that's on you. Yeah. Mike for Chris. If so, would you sign it if, like you said, it was it had the indemnify and hold harmless for the copywriter and indemnify and hold harmless, like, for each or just none. No, because I'm indemnifying him. Right. I'm fine with holding him harmless. I'm not going to sue anybody over anything, you know. There's, okay, here's the nightmare scenario. Okay, Guy comes in. You can sue anybody for anything at any time. Okay. And so Guy, a customer comes in and he, he sues everybody, everything that moves. He, he sues the company, he sues the, the owner individually. He sues me, he sues maybe the direct mail manager, sues everybody that was in the chain that got him to buy this product. Now, you know, that's going to cost us money, period. I mean, they may be frivolous lawsuits. They may be the kind of suits that are going to get thrown out of court the first time the judge sees them. But in the meantime, we're going to spend money on attorneys to, to get ready to fight it. So indemnification, I'm paying his legal bills. Right. And I'm not going to do that. So I'm fine holding him harmless, but I am not going to say, I'm not going to indemnify. Now, if, if an attorney sat down with me and explained this to me in plain English, I might have a different opinion. But as I understand it now, and I Googled it, I went online, I read on several legal sites, and to my best understanding, it's a real bad idea. So, and I've never done it. I, I just always say, no, I'm not going to do the indemnification stuff. All right. So if you don't understand something, ask, don't be afraid of looking dumb. Lawyers are specialists in this kind of stuff. They don't expect you to know this kind of stuff. So ask the questions. Use any clause that shows up in the contract that, that would, you didn't expect, you know. That's the time to slam the brakes on and say, wait a minute, guys, we didn't talk about this. If you're going to want that, then I'm going to want this. So anything above and beyond what you discussed prior to getting the contract, you know, is an opportunity for renegotiation and to make more money on the deal. So, and don't always assume that if something like that happens, it was like the clients of sneaky bastards. Sometimes things just don't come out in conversation, you know. So again, the benefit of the doubt and just figure a way to make money with it. Establish a relationship with a good lawyer. We talked about that. Never said anything you don't understand. Ask how compliance is handled. Better yet, have it a clause on compliance in here. I've had a lot of situations in Lane Lowry at Health Resources with one of them where when I first met him, I said, who does your compliance? And he goes, well, nobody really. I've got an attorney that's done some FDA work and every once in a while, he'll take a look at my stuff. And I said, well, if I work with you, you're going to have to agree that everything I write goes through that attorney and gets his blessing before we use it. Now, Lane was actually raided by the FDA. I mean, when they raid you, we're talking M16s and flak jackets, you know, and they can come in, they can grab all your computers, and it's just amazing what they can do. And he was raided and he still wasn't at that time, closely scrutinizing everywhere to copy that was going out. But when I came in, I insisted on it. And so everything Carlene and I did with him was went through compliance. So you may find yourself in that situation. Great company, great product, guy doesn't know beans about compliance. So you're going to have to say, look, we need to find somebody who used to work for the FDA. An attorney who used to work for the FDA would be best. Failing that, somebody who's defended cases against the FDA successfully. And of course, Lane was at Birmingham. Good luck finding a former FDA attorney there. But we found a good guy. And now on this, let's say you write some copy and the compliance people or the attorney come back to you and they go, oh, you can't say this, you can't say this. Don't just blindly delete the copy, you know, say, well, what can I say, you know, a good attorney will not tell you what you can't do. He'll tell you how to do what you want or need to do in order to be successful. So they should be identifying problems and then helping you solve them. So, you know, don't be intimidated by the attorney. He comes in and says, you can't use this word. Okay, what word can I use? You know, get their help with it. I have compliance people that try to rewrite copy and they can't even spell. You know, so you don't accept just everything. I mean, I've got this woman that is doing compliance at Wife. Wonderful lady, but she can't spell. And so she'll try to write a disclaimer and she goes, of course, you know, all investment carries risk and she spells course, C-O-A-R-S-E, you know. So, you know, don't just accept the changes at face value. You can negotiate and sometimes they'll read something wrong. You know, they'll read it the way they, you know, some unintended way and you can go back to them and say, well, that isn't what this passage actually says. Here's what it says. And so now, is that okay or do we need to make other changes? So be an active partner in this. Ultimately, it's their call. You're not in control there, but, you know, feel free to fight for your copy. Another way to go is to, you know, check with other copywriters who are signing contracts. Just talk to them if there's a clause you don't understand or maybe it's just a clause you're not sure is really normal in that industry. You know, call up somebody else that's writing in that industry and say, hey, do you have this in your contract? You know, and it'll let you know whether or not that's something you should seriously consider. And I like life signatures personally. There are new laws regarding virtual signatures that make them usable. Yeah, microphone back there for Barbara. If you were in negotiations with a company and they said they did not have a compliance department in place, would you walk? I've never had to do that because these companies don't want to go to jail either, you know. So what I have done is I've told them, look, you know, I can't work for you unless there's somebody doing compliance. And it doesn't have to be somebody internally. It can be just an attorney in town who will look at this stuff, right? We at Blanchard and Company, we went through a period of time. And this is just, this is aside from contracts, but it's interesting because it's on a legal basis. Be creative in the way that you address these legal and regulatory issues. Back in the 80s, the Federal Trade Commission went on a rampage against rare coin companies. They were dragging people into court right and left. They were putting some people in jail. It was, they just had a vendetta. They saw rare coins as an unregulated investment, which they really are. You know, if I'm selling stocks, I have, you know, this many regulations from the SEC I have to follow, right? I can sell gold coins. I can pretty much say anything I want to, so long as it's not inaccurate or misleading. And so they were just sick of that. So they decided they were going to make some examples and they started coming after rare coin companies, right and left. We did two things in response. So we wanted a creative solution to this. We were the leader. We were the big guy. If anybody was going to be made an example of it, it was going to be us. Now, we weren't doing any of the bad stuff, but like I said yesterday, you don't have to be. All it takes is the allegation from a government regulator that you're somehow out of compliance or you're somehow fraudulent, and that's going to go carry with you for a long time, especially now with the Internet, you know, and, you know, you look up a company. The company may be the first listing on the results page, and then there could be 10 pages of, is such and such a fraud, you know. So it's, and it's on there forever. So we wanted to, we wanted to avoid any problems. So we did two things. First thing was we required that every single word of copy be substantiated. There was any claim of fact at all, and it didn't even have to do with how much a coin had appreciated in value. It could be that, you know, we talked about the coin was struck at the Carson City Mint between these years. We had to have third party substantiation for that. So we had a substantiation file for every single, every single project that we did. And the way the government operates is you'll be at work one day and you'll get a phone call from the Federal Trade Commission, and they'll say, we're on the way to the courthouse. We're going to get a temporary restraining order against you. And what that means is you cannot conduct business. You have to basically shut down. You cannot promote. You cannot sell over the phone. You cannot conduct business if you have a TRO against you. Okay? So you're out of business. Now this is with no evidence. This is with no evidence, no hearing, nothing. They just say, we don't like the way you're doing things. We're going to go get a TRO. We wanted to be able to grab a file, beat them to the courthouse, slap the file on the judge's desk, and say every word in that promotion is true. And we got to the place where we could do that. So that was really important. The second thing that we did, and this was brilliant. I'm sorry, but it was really brilliant. You know how you watch these, they have these little spots on TV where there's something from the government, is it working now? From the government printing office offering you a free report on something, and some kind of consumer report on something. And the Colorado Springs thing, you call 800 number and you can get this free report from the government. So I started thinking, okay, why don't I write the definitive consumer guide to investing in rare coins, and have the government offer it, right? So then, in order to do this, you have to have a regulator, or an agency, the federal government, co-sponsor the report with you. So we were doing all of our promotions through the mail. So I picked the post office. So now I've got, there's no way anyone's going to come against me for mail fraud. I mean, I'm the guy who's so squeaky clean the post office co-sponsored my report, warning people about how coin companies rip them off. I mean, it was really great. It took like 60 days from the time we decided to do it to the time it was up there, and I think it's still there. I think if you go to the GPO site, you'd probably find it, a consumer's guide to rare coin investment. We were squeaky clean, but again, this really helped position us head and shoulders above the competition. So microphone for Chris. Two quick questions, Clayton. When you hold, or hold harmless a client, do you ask them to hold you harmless? Yes. And as, like, when we went through the contract, you could see that it was project-based. Yes. So every time new project, new contract is there ever a time where you kind of get into a big umbrella contract, or that just doesn't happen? No, no. I mean, I'm under an umbrella contract that renews every three years, or five years. I think my new one is five years. So in that contract, everything's covered, you know, in a very different contract. But a lot of these clauses are also in there, the non-competes, and hold harmless, and stuff like that. But yeah, no, when I hire a client, every time I hire you, Chris, you already signed a contract. Okay. Next time I hire you, you'll sign another, that same contract again. So each time. Yes. Microphone up here. Two quick questions. When you're starting out, and you're working with a big company, and they give you a contract that they use with other copywriters, and now that I've seen this, and possibly something is missing, or whatever, would I go back to them and say I want to have my own contract, or what would I do if they already have a standard one? I would identify any problematic areas. I tell you, did you read the contract that we sent you? Did you actually read it before you signed it? I did, yeah. And you still signed it? There was stuff in there that I wouldn't sign in a million years. You know, it was our own personnel department, and our own legal beagles, our own ambulance chasers, and they all got together and came up with this monstrosity that basically requires you to give us a list of every client you've ever had. I mean, there are all kinds of owners' clauses in there. And when I saw it for the first time last year, I freaked out, and I said, I would never sign this a million years. And if any copywriter ever asked me if they should sign it, I would tell them no. Not from you. I mean, talking about the company, the other company. Yeah, yeah. Well, I was talking to Chris too. Oh, oh, oh. Because she signed this thing. Oh, okay. So get a lawyer. No, but the answer is, my answer is don't be nitpicky at that point. If it's a quality company and you know they pay and they don't have a history of screwing copywriters, then only if there's a major point in there that you really don't like. There was a non-computer that isn't compensated or if there was an indemnification clause that puts you at great risk. But that's why you need to have an attorney look at it and just ask him. And pay for an hour of his time. It can be one of the cheapest things you ever do. Yes, microphone in the back. Oh, do you have another? Can I, very short. Sure. Do you also have liability, personal liability insurance for your copywriting? No. No, that contract covers it. Yeah, I don't feel I've ever needed it. I have a LLC, a limited liability corporation that does a fairly good job of, you know, there's a kind of a corporate shield there to protect us as individuals if something would happen. They are always, you can always penetrate those. The government especially can, but we've never felt a need for the insurance. Don't be nitpicky on the contract. You know, if there's some clauses in there that you have questions about but you can live with, live with them. You don't want to be problematic. But if there is a clause in there you cannot live with, then I would raise that issue with them. But again, I would go to an attorney first, determine exactly what your liability is from that clause. Or maybe it's not a liability. You know, maybe you look at the clause and as I said, it's non-compete. So it's something you can say, look, my fees were based on a one project thing only. If you want a non-compete, that's a different deal. We're going to have to work out compensation for it. So, but you can make a really good point. You don't want to be the problem child here, you know. So only if it's important, only if your attorney feels it's important.
